message:
perf(storage): cache index-writer busy_timeout PRAGMA across writer reuse

The index-writer apply path was unconditionally executing
`PRAGMA busy_timeout = N;` on every call to
`apply_index_writer_busy_timeout`, which fires for each cached
ephemeral writer checkout. The PRAGMA itself is cheap, but the
SQL parse + dispatch through FrankenSQLite for what is most often
a no-op (the cached writer was just released with the same value)
was visible in indexer hot-path profiling.

Mirrors the existing caching pattern for
`index_writer_checkpoint_pages`: track the most-recently-applied
value on `FrankenStorage`, and only re-apply the PRAGMA when the
desired value differs from the cached one. The cache survives
release-back-to-pool / checkout-from-pool round trips because the
state is now carried on `SendFrankenConnection` alongside the
checkpoint-pages state, and is restored onto the materialized
writer when it's pulled back out.

Changes:

1. **`FrankenStorage::index_writer_busy_timeout_ms`** —
   New `AtomicU64` field with sentinel `UNSET_INDEX_WRITER_BUSY_
   TIMEOUT_MS = 0` (zero is invalid as a real timeout, so it's a
   safe out-of-band marker). Two new accessors,
   `index_writer_busy_timeout_ms()` and
   `mark_index_writer_busy_timeout_ms()`, mirror the names used
   for the checkpoint-pages cache and use the same `Ordering::
   Relaxed` semantics (this is a hint, not a synchronization
   primitive — incorrect concurrent reads would just cause one
   redundant PRAGMA, never break correctness).

2. **`SendFrankenConnection` carries the cached state.** Until
   now, `SendFrankenConnection` was a one-tuple wrapper around
   `FrankenConnection`. Extended to a 3-tuple of
   `(FrankenConnection, checkpoint_pages: i64, busy_timeout_ms:
   u64)` so the cached writer's tuned PRAGMA values follow the
   connection through release/reuse. Added two helpers:

   - `new(conn)` — initializes both extra fields to their
     UNSET sentinels (used by every existing call site and the
     reader-pool factory; nothing changes for those paths).
   - `new_with_index_writer_state(conn, pages, ms)` — used by
     `release_cached_ephemeral_writer` to record the live
     PRAGMA state at release time so the next checkout can
     restore it without re-issuing the PRAGMA.
   - `into_parts()` (replaces the old `into_inner()`) returns
     all three fields. The single caller of the old
     `into_inner()` is the cache-checkout path, which is the
     only consumer of the extra state.

3. **`apply_index_writer_busy_timeout`** (in `src/indexer/mod.rs`):
   Short-circuits when
   `storage.index_writer_busy_timeout_ms() == Some(busy_timeout_ms)`
   — i.e. the desired value matches what was last applied. On
   the cold path, it executes the PRAGMA as before, and on
   success calls `mark_index_writer_busy_timeout_ms(...)` to
   prime the cache. Failure path is unchanged: the same debug
   trace fires and the cache is intentionally not updated, so
   the next attempt retries the PRAGMA.

4. **`acquire_cached_ephemeral_writer`** restores both cached
   PRAGMA values onto the materialized writer via the new
   `into_parts()` and stores them with `Ordering::Relaxed`
   atomic stores. The writer therefore enters service with the
   `index_writer_busy_timeout_ms` cache primed, and the
   `apply_*` short-circuit fires on the next call instead of
   re-issuing the PRAGMA.

5. **`release_cached_ephemeral_writer`** snapshots both atomic
   counters before consuming the writer, then constructs the
   `SendFrankenConnection` via
   `new_with_index_writer_state(conn, checkpoint_pages,
   busy_timeout_ms)` so the values travel back into the cache
   slot.

No public API change. The reader pool path (which calls
`SendFrankenConnection::new` directly) keeps the UNSET sentinels;
readers don't apply the index-writer PRAGMAs and don't need this
cache state.

Steady-state effect: indexer churn workloads that repeatedly
acquire/release the cached writer stop paying for one PRAGMA
parse-and-execute per round trip. The single PRAGMA exec only
runs when the configured `index_writer_busy_timeout_ms()` is
actually changed at runtime (env-var hot-reload, test override),
or on the first acquire after process start.
